text: The surgeon begins the dissection using a fenestrated bipolar instrument in the left hand and a monopolar hook in the right hand, attempting to find a plane between the inferior aspect of the liver and the omentum. The dissection involves swiping movements for blunt dissection and short bursts of energy to maintain the correct plane and avoid injuries.

The surgeon creates a dissection plane inferiorly, staying close to the gallbladder while pushing down surrounding fat with steady traction. The duodenum is observed in an unusually high position, leading to cautery injury, which necessitates repair to prevent leakage.

The surgeon clears the omentum from the area around the gallbladder fundus, revealing a small knuckle of duodenum attached, indicating a cholecystoid duodenal fistula. The surgeon repairs the area that received cautery injury using a 3-0 PDS suture and a needle driver, placing interrupted stitches to ensure proper healing and prevent leakage.

The surgeon continues dissection around the fistulous tract extending from the duodenum to the fundus of the gallbladder, using a robotic clip applicator for retraction. The Firefly mode is deployed to delineate the biliary tree, revealing the obstruction at the gallbladder neck and highlighting the duodenum side. An articulating stapler, the SureForm stapler from the Da Vinci system, is visible in use.

The surgeon uses a three-row stapler to secure the tissue, avoiding reinforcement stitches or burying the staple line. The dissection continues in the thickened, vascular area due to chronic inflammation, which appears oozy. The surgeon identifies anatomical landmarks such as the cystic artery, cystic duct, and lymph node of the lung, using firefly mode for guidance. Despite no delineation, the surgeon enters the gallbladder, releasing mucus, and proceeds with a subtotal cholecystectomy to safely transect the neck of the gallbladder, minimizing risk to surrounding structures.

The surgeon clears debris from the neck region and cystic duct junction using suction irrigation, creating a stump for closure. A V-lock suture is used to secure the stump with running stitches in a vertical fashion, accommodating friable and inflamed tissue. The gallbladder remnant is dissected off the liver bed using short bursts of current and constant traction, with careful maneuvering of instruments from various angles to open the plane in the scarred area.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to detach the final piece of tissue connecting the gallbladder to the liver, completing the removal of the gallbladder.
